_meta.authoringNotes: Fischer K et al. Tungiasis review Trop Med Int Health 2009. Heukelbach J et al. Tungiasis-related morbidity in northeast Brazil. Feldmeier H et al. WHO position on tungiasis 2022. Pilz D et al. Tungiasis treatment literature.

h1Title: Tungiasis (Sandflea Infestation): Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis and Treatment
heroOverview: Tungiasis is a zoonotic ectoparasitosis caused by the female sand flea Tunga penetrans (also known as Tunga penetrans, Jiggers, or nigua), a small (1mm) flea native to the Caribbean and Latin America that has spread to sub-Saharan Africa and parts of South Asia. The female flea burrows headfirst into the epidermis of the host (usually the feet — particularly the toe web spaces, heels, and subungual areas), where she engorges with blood, grows up to 1cm in diameter, and produces eggs that are expelled onto the ground. The infestation causes intense pruritus, pain, inflammation, and risk of secondary bacterial infection, which can lead to severe complications including gangrene, tetanus, and auto-amputation of digits in severe cases. Extremely common in rural endemic areas — prevalence reaches 50-80% of inhabitants in some communities in East Africa, particularly in children and the elderly living in extreme poverty without shoes. The disease is associated with significant disability and lost school days. Treatment involves physical extraction of the flea with a sterile needle or scalpel followed by antiseptic wound care, along with tetanus prophylaxis. Ivermectin and topical anti-parasitic agents have been studied with mixed results.

keyStats.prevalence: Prevalence 50-80% in endemic rural communities in East Africa; 40-60% in Caribbean/Latin American endemic areas
keyStats.demographics: Children 5-15 years most commonly affected; elderly with limited mobility; equal sex distribution
keyStats.avgAge: Peak burden in children 5-14 years; second peak in elderly >65 years
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 1-2 million cases in sub-Saharan Africa alone; considered a neglected tropical disease by WHO with significant disability burden

definition: Tungiasis (ICD-10: B88.1) is infestation by the sand flea Tunga penetrans (order Siphonaptera, family Tungidae), a 1mm parasitic insect that infests humans and animals (pigs, rats, dogs, cats). The life cycle involves adult fleas living in sandy soil; the female, after mating, jumps onto a host and burrows into the epidermis headfirst, typically on the feet — the only body part in contact with infested sandy soil. The flea embeds in the stratum granulosum, feeds on blood by inserting its proboscis into dermal capillaries, and over 2-3 weeks grows into a fully engorged state (up to 1cm diameter) visible as a white or yellowish nodule with a central dark punctum (the flea's respiratory and egg-laying orifice). The flea produces 100-200 eggs over 2-3 weeks, expelled through the punctum onto the ground where larvae develop. After egg-laying, the flea dies in situ and is gradually expelled or absorbed. The lesion is called a 'burrow' or 'tungoma' and causes intense local inflammation, pruritus, and pain. Secondary bacterial infection (Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pyogenes) causes cellulitis, abscess formation, lymphangitis, and in severe cases, gangrene and tetanus. Chronic tungiasis causes disfiguring nail deformation, toe deformities, and functional impairment of walking. Medical specialties managing tungiasis include infectious disease, dermatology, and podiatry.

typesClassification.description: Within the first 24-48 hours after flea penetration. Minimal visible change; slight erythema and intense pruritus. Difficult to detect clinically.

The classic lesion: a 2-10mm white/yellowish nodule with a central black/brown punctum (the flea's posterior end). Surrounding erythema, edema, pain, and intense pruritus. Eggs being expelled from punctum.

Secondary bacterial infection of the lesion causes cellulitis, abscess formation, foul discharge, pain, and systemic symptoms (fever, malaise). May lead to lymphangitis, regional lymphadenitis, and sepsis.

Long-standing tungiasis with multiple lesions, fibrosis, nail deformity, toe disfigurement, and persistent secondary infection. Associated with significant disability — difficulty walking, standing, and performing daily activities.

primarySymptoms: Intense pruritus — the hallmark symptom; often described as the most troublesome aspect; worsens at night
Pain and tenderness — particularly when lesions are on weight-bearing areas of the feet
Papule/nodule on the foot — typically 2-10mm, white/yellow with central dark punctum; may appear inflamed and erythematous
Edema and erythema around the lesion — local inflammatory response to the embedded flea
Fissuring and skin breakdown around lesions — particularly in chronic cases or when multiple lesions coalesce
Foul odor from infected lesions — bacterial superinfection with foul-smelling discharge
Discoloration and deformation of toenails — chronic tungiasis causes nail plate distortion and subungual lesions
Difficulty walking — pain and inflammation limit mobility, especially in children and elderly
Lesion on the heel, toe web, or sole — the most common locations; lesions on subungual areas cause more pain
Acute inflammation (acute phase) — lesions become angry red, swollen, and may suppurate
earlyWarningSigns: Multiple lesions (>10) with pain and difficulty walking in a child from endemic rural area — severe tungiasis requiring urgent treatment
Signs of bacterial cellulitis (rapidly spreading erythema, warmth, fever) — indicates secondary bacterial infection requiring antibiotics
Lesions near the nail fold with nail deformity — subungual tungiasis is particularly painful and prone to secondary infection
Lesions with foul discharge and surrounding inflammation — possible abscess requiring drainage
Tetanus-prone wound (dirty, puncture) in a patient with multiple tungiasis lesions — tetanus prophylaxis required
emergencySigns: Rapidly spreading cellulitis, fever, and regional lymphangitis — spreading bacterial infection requiring IV antibiotics
Gas gangrene or rapidly progressive necrotizing infection — rare but life-threatening complication of heavily infected tungiasis
Septicemia from heavily infected tungiasis — high fever, hypotension, altered mental status
Tetanus infection from wound contamination — lockjaw, opisthotonus, and generalized spasms
Severe disfigurement with toe necrosis — may require surgical amputation in worst cases

causes.description: Female sand fleas live in sandy soil of endemic areas; they jump onto bare feet and burrow headfirst into the epidermis. The flea requires host blood for egg production — each flea is introduced by a single penetration event.

causes.cause: Walking barefoot in infested sandy areas
causes.description: The flea lives in dry, sandy soil — beaches, household yards, paths in endemic areas. Walking barefoot is the primary mode of transmission. Bare feet on sand = direct contact with infested soil.

causes.cause: Domestic animals (pigs, dogs, rats) as flea reservoirs
causes.description: T. penetrans infects a wide range of mammals — pigs are the main reservoir in many endemic areas, followed by dogs, cats, and rats. Infested animals deposit eggs in the soil, maintaining the environmental flea population.

causes.cause: Poor sanitation and sandy household environments
causes.description: Sandy, dry soil around homes (from dirt floors, unpaved yards) provides ideal habitat for T. penetrans larvae to develop. Poor housing (no concrete floors, thatched roofs) increases exposure.

riskFactors.description: The single most important risk factor — fleas are acquired from walking on infested sand/soil without footwear. Children and elderly who cannot or do not wear shoes are at highest risk.
Rural communities in endemic areas have 50-80% prevalence vs. near-zero in urban areas. Proximity to domestic animals (pigs) and sandy soil provide ideal transmission conditions.
Children have the highest prevalence — they play barefoot in sand, have more skin contact with ground, and may not have access to shoes. School absenteeism from tungiasis is a documented problem.
Elderly people who cannot easily put on/removes shoes, and those who are homebound in endemic areas, have high infection rates. Reduced skin integrity and immunity also contribute.
Dirt floors, lack of concrete flooring, keeping pigs inside or near the house — all create and maintain flea populations. Poverty directly drives tungiasis prevalence.

affectedDemographics: Children aged 5-15 years in endemic rural communities — highest prevalence and burden of disease
Elderly and mobility-impaired individuals in endemic areas — second peak of burden
People living in extreme poverty with dirt floors and domestic animals in the household
Agricultural workers and subsistence farmers in endemic regions
diagnosisOverview: Tungiasis is a clinical diagnosis based on the characteristic appearance of the lesion — a white/yellow nodule with central dark punctum on the feet of a person from or recently returned from an endemic area. The differential diagnosis includes pyogenic granuloma, plantar warts, myiasis (fly larvae infestation), and foreign body granuloma. The key diagnostic feature is the central dark punctum — the posterior end of the flea — visible as a dark dot in the center of the lesion. When in doubt, the flea can be extracted with a sterile needle and identified macroscopically (dark posterior end, lateral legs visible). Skin scrapings are not particularly useful. A thorough examination of both feet (including toe webs, soles, and subungual areas) should be done, as multiple lesions are common. Laboratory studies are not routinely needed unless secondary bacterial infection is suspected (CBC, wound culture, blood culture if systemic infection). Tetanus immunization status should be assessed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Visual diagnosis of tungiasis based on characteristic lesion — white/yellow nodule with central dark punctum. Examination of entire foot including toe webs.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Visual inspection with good light. Characteristic 'tungoma' with central punctum confirms diagnosis. Multiple lesions common — count and document extent.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirm diagnosis in ambiguous cases by extracting the flea and identifying it under magnification.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Sterile needle or scalpel used to extract the flea. Dark posterior end with visible legs under magnification. Can be done at bedside.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identify bacterial co-infection when cellulitis, abscess, or foul discharge is present.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Swab of purulent discharge or aspirate from abscess. Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus pyogenes most common; consider polymicrobial infection.

diagnosticTests.purpose: CBC, CRP, blood culture if systemic toxicity (fever, malaise, sepsis) is present.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: CBC shows leukocytosis in bacterial superinfection. Blood culture positive in septicemia. CRP guides severity of inflammatory response.

treatmentOverview: The cornerstone of tungiasis treatment is physical extraction of the flea followed by antiseptic wound care. Extraction is performed with a sterile needle or scalpel — the flea is gently pushed out from the embedded position, taking care not to crush it and release eggs into the wound. Antiseptic cleaning (povidone-iodine or chlorhexidine) follows, with a topical antibiotic ointment. Systemic antibiotics (clindamycin, amoxicillin-clavulanate, or cotrimoxazole) are required for extensive secondary bacterial infection, cellulitis, or abscess. Tetanus prophylaxis is essential — assess vaccination status and give Td/Tdap booster if needed. Topical ivermectin, dimeticone (silicon-based occlusive), and oral ivermectin have been studied with mixed results — none are standard of care yet. Heavy infestations with dozens of lesions may require referral to a health facility for planned extraction. Environmental management — wearing shoes, concrete floors, keeping animals away from living areas — is the only sustainable prevention. WHO has identified tungiasis as a neglected tropical disease requiring improved surveillance and control programs. Heavy infestations with dozens of lesions may require referral to a health facility for planned extraction under sterile conditions. WHO has identified tungiasis as a neglected tropical disease requiring improved surveillance and control programs in endemic regions. Topical ivermectin, dimeticone, and oral ivermectin have been studied with mixed results — none are standard of care yet, but dimeticone shows promising 70-80% cure rates.

medicalTreatments.name: Physical extraction of flea + antiseptic wound care
medicalTreatments.description: The definitive treatment for each individual lesion. Sterile needle or scalpel is used to extract the flea; the wound is then cleaned with povidone-iodine and a topical antibiotic ointment (mupirocin or fusidic acid) applied.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Extraction relieves symptoms immediately and prevents further egg production. Complete cure of individual lesion within 1-2 weeks. Prevents secondary infection if done promptly. No drug resistance concern.
medicalTreatments.typicalCourse: One-time extraction per lesion. Repeat extraction if new lesions appear. Ongoing wound care 2-3 times daily with antiseptic and antibiotic ointment.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical mupirocin or fusidic acid ointment
medicalTreatments.description: Topical antibiotic applied after flea extraction to prevent and treat secondary bacterial infection of the wound.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective against Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA) and Streptococcus pyogenes superficial wound infections. Accelerates wound healing.
medicalTreatments.typicalCourse: Apply to each lesion 2-3 times daily after cleaning. Continue until wound heals (usually 3-7 days). For multiple lesions, apply to all lesions.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral antibiotics for extensive secondary infection/cellulitis
medicalTreatments.description: Systemic antibiotics for tungiasis with extensive secondary infection, cellulitis, lymphangitis, or abscess.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical resolution of bacterial superinfection in 5-7 days. Antibiotic choice based on likely organisms (Staph aureus, Strep pyogenes).
medicalTreatments.typicalCourse: Clindamycin 300 mg 3 times daily for 5-7 days, or amoxicillin-clavulanate 625 mg 3 times daily for 5-7 days. Severe infection: IV clindamycin + ceftriaxone.

medicalTreatments.name: Tetanus prophylaxis (Td/Tdap booster)
medicalTreatments.description: Tungiasis lesions are puncture wounds that can be contaminated with Clostridium tetani spores from soil. Tetanus prophylaxis is a critical component of management.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents tetanus — a life-threatening complication of contaminated wounds. Essential even for minor lesions.
medicalTreatments.typicalCourse: If vaccination history unknown or >10 years since last booster: Tdap booster. If wound is heavily contaminated or >6 hours old: Tdap + tetanus immunoglobulin (TIG) 250 IU IM.

surgicalOptions.name: Incision and drainage of abscess
surgicalOptions.description: Incision and drainage (I&D) of fluctuant abscesses complicating heavily infected tungiasis. Often requires examination under anesthesia for multiple abscesses.
surgicalOptions.whatToExpect: Lancing and draining of pus under sterile conditions. Wound packed and left to heal by secondary intention. Antibiotics and daily wound care follow.

surgicalOptions.name: Debridement of necrotic tissue
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal of dead/necrotic tissue in severe chronic tungiasis with gangrene. May ultimately require toe amputation in the worst cases.
surgicalOptions.whatToExpect: Sharp debridement of necrotic tissue. Limited amputation if toes are necrotic and non-viable. Reconstruction if possible; otherwise healing by secondary intention.

alternativeTreatments.name: Topical dimeticone (occlusive silicone gel)
alternativeTreatments.description: A silicon-based occlusive gel that suffocates embedded fleas by blocking their respiratory and egg-laying orifices. Applied as a thick layer over lesions.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Studies in Tanzania showed cure rates of 70-80% with twice-daily application for 7 days. Simple, safe, and can be applied by community health workers.
alternativeTreatments.typicalCourse: Apply thick layer of dimeticone gel over all lesions twice daily for 7 days. Fleas die and are expelled. Repeat if new lesions appear.

alternativeTreatments.name: Topical ivermectin (2% solution)
alternativeTreatments.description: Ivermectin is an antiparasitic that kills T. penetrans when applied topically. Studied as an alternative to extraction.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Some studies show 60-70% cure rates with topical ivermectin. Less effective than physical extraction but less painful.
alternativeTreatments.typicalCourse: 2% ivermectin solution applied to lesions twice daily for 7 days.

alternativeTreatments.name: Oral ivermectin
alternativeTreatments.description: A systemic antiparasitic that may kill embedded T. penetrans. Studied in East African trials with mixed results.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Mixed results — some trials showed reduction in lesion count; others showed no significant benefit over control. Not yet established as standard of care.
alternativeTreatments.typicalCourse: 200 mcg/kg oral single dose or repeated after 2 weeks. Used in combination with topical dimeticone in some protocols.

whySeeSpecialist: Most tungiasis can be managed by a primary healthcare worker or community health volunteer trained in flea extraction. Specialist referral is needed for: severe cases with extensive secondary infection requiring IV antibiotics and surgical drainage (infectious disease/general surgery), necrotic tissue requiring debridement (surgery), toe amputation in severe disfigurement (orthopedic surgery), and management of chronic disability and rehabilitation (dermatology, podiatry).
doctorSelectionGuide: In endemic areas, community health workers trained in tungiasis management are the first point of care. Health centers can handle extraction and wound care. District hospitals handle severe cellulitis, abscess drainage, and surgical debridement. For travelers returning from endemic areas, a primary care physician or dermatologist can manage the condition.
hospitalCriteria: Primary health center with trained staff for flea extraction and wound care
Surgical capability for abscess drainage and debridement
Laboratory for wound culture if secondary bacterial infection is extensive
Community health outreach for environmental management and shoe distribution
keyFacilities: Trained community health workers for active case finding and treatment in endemic villages
Surgical theater for debridement and minor amputations in severe cases
Wound care supplies: sterile needles, antiseptic solutions, topical antibiotics, dressing materials
Tetanus prophylaxis (Tdap vaccine, TIG) for wound management
Epidemiology/surveillance system to track tungiasis burden in endemic districts

costBreakdown.consultation: Community health worker visit: free in most endemic country programs. Health center: ₹20-₹200.
costBreakdown.diagnosticTests: Clinical diagnosis — no tests needed for typical cases. Wound culture: ₹100-₹500.
costBreakdown.medications: Topical mupirocin: ₹30-₹150. Povidone-iodine: ₹30-₹100. Oral antibiotics: ₹50-₹300. Tetanus toxoid: free in most programs.

insuranceGuide: Tungiasis is covered under basic health insurance in endemic countries. In India, Ayushman Bharat PMJAY covers hospitalization for severe infections. Neglected tropical disease programs in many countries provide free treatment.
financialAssistance: Treatment is inexpensive and often free through government health programs in endemic countries. Shoe distribution programs and housing improvement schemes are the most impactful long-term interventions. WHO NTD program supports tungiasis control in endemic countries.
preventionStrategies: Wearing shoes (closed footwear, sandals) at all times in endemic areas — the single most effective prevention
Concrete flooring instead of dirt floors in homes — eliminates flea habitat in living areas
Keeping domestic animals (pigs, dogs) away from living and sleeping areas
Regular inspection of feet for embedded fleas with prompt extraction
Applying dimeticone gel to feet as prophylaxis in endemic communities
Health education about tungiasis and its prevention in endemic schools and communities
lifestyleModifications: Wear closed shoes or sandals at all times when walking in endemic areas — even in the house
Check feet daily for embedded fleas, especially between toes and on heels
Replace dirt floors with concrete or tiles in home environments
Keep animals outside or away from sleeping areas — reduce flea reservoir in immediate environment
Apply preventive topical agents (dimeticone, permethrin) to feet before going to endemic areas
Regular washing of feet and inspection of toe webs for early lesions

recommended: High-protein diet to support wound healing and immune function
Vitamin C-rich foods (citrus, guava) for collagen synthesis and wound repair
Iron-rich foods if chronic blood loss from infected lesions is present
Nutrient-dense diet for children to support growth and immunity
avoid: No specific food restrictions for tungiasis — general healthy diet is appropriate
Traditional remedies applied to lesions without antiseptic cleaning (may worsen infection)

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is not appropriate during active tungiasis with multiple painful lesions, especially on weight-bearing surfaces. Rest with foot elevation helps reduce inflammation. After treatment and wound healing, normal activities can resume. Prevention through footwear is the only effective exercise-related strategy.
dailyManagement: Inspect feet daily for new embedded fleas — early detection and extraction prevents progression
Extract fleas promptly with a sterile needle, then clean with povidone-iodine and apply topical antibiotic
Keep feet clean and dry — moisture worsens bacterial superinfection
Wear closed shoes at all times in endemic areas — even indoors
Seek care at a health center if lesions show signs of bacterial infection (redness spreading, pus, fever)
Ensure tetanus vaccination is up to date
Keep children from playing barefoot in infested areas
Report heavy infestations (many lesions) to community health worker for mass treatment
prognosis: With prompt flea extraction and wound care, individual lesions resolve within 1-2 weeks without complications. Heavily infested patients (dozens of lesions) may have persistent discomfort and disability for weeks to months. Secondary bacterial infection is the main cause of morbidity — cellulitis, abscess, and in severe cases, gangrene or tetanus can be life-threatening. Chronic tungiasis causes permanent toe and nail deformities and functional impairment. With proper prevention (shoes, concrete floors), tungiasis is entirely preventable. Secondary bacterial infection is the main cause of morbidity — cellulitis, abscess, and in severe cases, gangrene or tetanus can be life-threatening. With proper prevention measures (shoes, concrete floors), tungiasis is entirely preventable in endemic communities.
recoveryTimeline: Individual lesion healing: 1-2 weeks after flea extraction with antiseptic care. Multiple lesions: improvement over 2-4 weeks with regular extraction and wound care. Return to normal walking when inflammation resolves (2-4 weeks). Chronic deformities may be permanent without surgical correction.
complications: Secondary bacterial infection — cellulitis, abscess, lymphangitis, and potentially bacteremia/sepsis
Tetanus — from wound contamination with Clostridium tetani in heavily infested lesions
Gangrene and tissue necrosis — from severe infection causing vascular compromise to toes
Toe deformity and nail destruction — chronic tungiasis causes permanent nail plate damage and fibrotic toe changes
Auto-amputation of digits — in the most severe cases, toes may be lost due to gangrene and tissue death
Disability and impaired quality of life — difficulty walking, standing, and working; school absenteeism in children

supportResources.name: WHO - Neglected Tropical Diseases: Tungiasis
supportResources.description: WHO information on tungiasis as a neglected tropical disease, including burden data and control strategies

supportResources.name: Centre for Disease Control - Tungiasis Information
supportResources.description: CDC guidance on tungiasis diagnosis, treatment, and prevention for travelers and healthcare providers

supportResources.name: FIND Neglected Skin Diseases Programme
supportResources.description: Research and control programs for tungiasis in endemic countries including shoe distribution and environmental management

confusedWithConditions.name: Plantar warts (verruca plantaris)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Plantar warts are caused by HPV and appear as rough, hyperkeratotic papules with pinpoint black dots (thrombosed capillaries) — not a central punctum with flea characteristics. Warts are not painful to the same degree and are not associated with the living environment risk factors of tungiasis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cutaneous myiasis (fly larvae infestation)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Myiasis from Cordylobia anthropophaga (tumbu fly) or Dermatobia hominis causes boil-like lesions with central breathing hole, but the maggots are visible and mobile within the lesion — different from an embedded flea. Myiasis larvae are larger and can be extracted with gentle pressure.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pyogenic granuloma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pyogenic granuloma is a rapidly growing red/purple vascular lesion that bleeds easily on contact, occurring after minor trauma. It has no central punctum and doesn't appear in the typical sandflea distribution pattern. No connection to endemic exposure.

coOccurringConditions.name: Tetanus
coOccurringConditions.reason: Tungiasis lesions are puncture wounds contaminated with soil — a risk for tetanus infection if vaccination is not up to date. Tetanus is a life-threatening complication of heavily infested tungiasis.

coOccurringConditions.name: Buruli ulcer
coOccurringConditions.reason: Both are neglected tropical diseases of poverty causing skin lesions in West Africa. Co-endemic in many areas. Different etiologies — Buruli is Mycobacterium ulcerans infection.

coOccurringConditions.name: Scabies
coOccurringConditions.reason: Both are ectoparasites causing intense pruritus. Co-infection with scabies and tungiasis is common in endemic communities, making clinical diagnosis more complex.

relatedConditions.name: Myiasis (cutaneous)
relatedConditions.relationship: Another ectoparasite causing skin lesions in endemic areas — fly larvae rather than flea; similar transmission from soil/organic matter

relatedConditions.name: Tetanus
relatedConditions.relationship: A complication of heavily infected tungiasis — contaminated puncture wounds allow Clostridium tetani entry

relatedConditions.name: Bacterial cellulitis
relatedConditions.relationship: A common complication of tungiasis with secondary bacterial infection — requires systemic antibiotics

faqs.question: What is tungiasis and what causes it?
faqs.answer: Tungiasis is an infestation of the skin caused by a tiny sand flea called Tunga penetrans. The female flea burrows into the skin — usually on the feet — and grows inside a small lump. It causes intense itching, pain, and can get infected. The fleas live in sandy soil in tropical areas, and people get them by walking barefoot.

faqs.question: How do I know if I have tungiasis?
faqs.answer: You'll notice a small, itchy bump on your foot — usually between the toes, on the heel, or around the toenails. The bump is white or yellow with a tiny dark dot in the center (that's the flea's back end). It itches and hurts, especially when walking. The bump can grow to be quite large. If you live in or have visited an area where people walk barefoot in sandy soil, this is the most likely explanation.

faqs.question: How is tungiasis treated?
faqs.answer: The flea is removed with a clean needle or pointed scalpel — the doctor or health worker pushes it gently out of the skin. Then the wound is cleaned with antiseptic (like iodine) and an antibiotic ointment is applied. A tetanus shot is important since the wound can get tetanus. If there is a lot of infection, you may need antibiotics by mouth. Wearing shoes and keeping feet clean prevents getting it again.

faqs.question: Can I get tungiasis from another person?
faqs.answer: No, tungiasis is not contagious from person to person. You can only get it by being directly exposed to the sand fleas in infested soil. The fleas jump onto your feet when you walk barefoot on sand or dirt that contains the fleas.

faqs.question: What happens if tungiasis is not treated?
faqs.answer: Without treatment, the flea eventually dies and the lump goes away on its own — but this takes weeks. Meanwhile, the lesion can get badly infected with bacteria, causing swelling, pus, pain, and sometimes spreading infection up the leg. In severe cases, toes can become gangrenous and may need to be cut off. The intense itching also causes children to miss school.

faqs.question: How can I prevent tungiasis?
faqs.answer: The best prevention is wearing shoes at all times — even in the house. Concrete floors in homes prevent fleas from living in the soil. Keeping pigs and dogs away from living areas also reduces fleas. Checking feet daily for early fleas and removing them right away prevents the infestation from getting worse.

faqs.question: Is tungiasis dangerous?
faqs.answer: For most people, tungiasis is uncomfortable but not life-threatening — it causes pain, itching, and difficulty walking. However, heavy infestations with many fleas can lead to serious bacterial infections, gangrene, and even tetanus, which can be fatal. In poor communities where people can't access treatment, tungiasis causes real disability.

faqs.question: My child has many small itchy bumps on the feet. Could it be tungiasis?
faqs.answer: Yes — multiple lesions on the feet in children from endemic areas is the classic presentation of heavy tungiasis. Each bump is an embedded flea. With many fleas, children may have difficulty walking, miss school, and develop secondary infections. All fleas should be extracted and a health worker should check for infection.

faqs.question: Where in the world is tungiasis found?
faqs.answer: Tungiasis originated in the Caribbean and Latin America, but it spread to sub-Saharan Africa (particularly Tanzania, Kenya, Uganda, Nigeria, Ghana) and has been reported in parts of South Asia. It is most common in very poor rural communities where people walk barefoot on sandy soil, have dirt floors, and keep pigs near the house.

faqs.question: Why do pigs matter in tungiasis?
faqs.answer: Pigs are the main animal reservoir for Tunga penetrans — female fleas lay eggs in the soil, and pigs wandering in the same area pick up fleas and deposit eggs back into the household environment. Keeping pigs in or near the house creates a continuous source of fleas for humans to get.

faqs.question: What should I do if I find a flea embedded in my foot?
faqs.answer: Use a sterile needle or clean pointed object to gently push the flea out — it should come out whole. Don't squeeze it hard as this may rupture the flea and release eggs. Clean the wound with antiseptic (povidone-iodine is ideal) and apply antibiotic ointment. Cover with a clean bandage. Get a tetanus booster if your vaccination is not up to date.

faqs.question: Is there a medicine that kills the fleas without extracting them?
faqs.answer: Research is ongoing — studies show that applying dimeticone gel (a silicone-based gel) to the lesions twice daily for a week can suffocate the fleas and they fall out naturally. Oral ivermectin has been studied but results are mixed. Physical extraction remains the most reliable method. If you have many lesions, see a health worker for help.

metaDescription: Expert guide to tungiasis (sandflea/jiggers): flea extraction, secondary infection treatment, prevention. For endemic communities and healthcare workers.
